title: AU: Civil Construction Induction
subtitle: Entry-Level Safety and Compliance — Australia
description: Essential knowledge for anyone starting in Australian civil construction. Covers the WHS Act, Safe Work Australia, HRW licensing, White Card, and Austroads standards.

lessons.title: Work Health and Safety Act 2011 — PCBU, Workers and Regulators
lessons.content:
## Work Health and Safety Act 2011 (WHS Act)

The Work Health and Safety Act 2011 is Australia's harmonised workplace health and safety legislation. Developed by Safe Work Australia, model WHS laws were designed to create consistent national standards across participating jurisdictions, replacing a patchwork of state-based legislation.

## Harmonisation — Which Jurisdictions Adopted the Model WHS Laws?

| Jurisdiction | Adoption Status |
|-------------|----------------|
| Commonwealth | WHS Act 2011 |
| NSW | WHS Act 2011 |
| Queensland | WHS Act 2011 |
| South Australia | WHS Act 2012 |
| Tasmania | WHS Act 2012 |
| ACT | WHS Act 2011 |
| NT | WHS Act 2011 |
| Victoria | OHS Act 2004 (not harmonised) |
| Western Australia | WHS Act 2020 (adopted model laws 2022) |

**Safe Work Australia** is the national policy body — it develops the model laws and codes of practice but does NOT enforce them. Enforcement is the responsibility of each state/territory regulator.

## PCBU — Person Conducting a Business or Undertaking

The **PCBU** is the primary duty holder under the WHS Act — essentially any business entity:
- Companies, partnerships, sole traders
- Contractors, subcontractors, and sub-subcontractors
- Labour hire companies
- Franchisors

**Primary duty (s.19):** A PCBU must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, the health and safety of workers it engages AND others who may be affected by its work activities.

## Workers Under the WHS Act

The definition of 'worker' under the WHS Act is broad:
- **Employees** (full-time, part-time, casual)
- **Contractors and sub-contractors**
- **Labour hire workers**
- **Apprentices and trainees**
- **Students on work experience**
- **Volunteers** (in some circumstances)

Workers also have duties — they must:
- Take reasonable care for their own health and safety
- Take reasonable care not to adversely affect others
- Comply with reasonable instructions from the PCBU
- Co-operate with PCBU policies and procedures

## Officers — Due Diligence Duty

**Officers** (company directors, partners, senior managers) have a personal due diligence duty (s.27):
- Acquire and keep up-to-date knowledge of WHS matters
- Understand the organisation's operations and WHS hazards
- Ensure appropriate resources and processes
- Receive and consider information about incidents and hazards
- Verify that safety management systems are working

Officers can be prosecuted personally and independently of any PCBU prosecution.

## State Regulators

| Regulator | Jurisdiction |
|-----------|-------------|
| SafeWork NSW | New South Wales |
| WorkSafe Queensland | Queensland |
| SafeWork SA | South Australia |
| WorkSafe WA | Western Australia |
| WorkSafe Victoria | Victoria (OHS Act) |
| WorkSafe ACT | Australian Capital Territory |
| NT WorkSafe | Northern Territory |

Regulators have inspection and enforcement powers: improvement notices, prohibition notices, prosecutions.

## Reasonably Practicable

'Reasonably practicable' is the key standard throughout the WHS Act. It requires weighing:
1. Likelihood of the hazard or risk occurring
2. Degree of harm that could result
3. Knowledge about the hazard and ways to eliminate/minimise it
4. Availability and suitability of controls
5. Cost — only after the above four factors are considered

## Key Takeaways

- WHS Act 2011: harmonised national legislation — most states adopted, Victoria and WA delayed
- PCBU = central duty holder — almost every business qualifies
- Workers: broad definition including contractors, labour hire, trainees, volunteers
- Officers have personal due diligence duties — can be prosecuted independently
- Reasonably practicable: cost is the LAST factor — not the first justification

questions.q: Under the WHS Act 2011, which body develops the model WHS laws but does NOT enforce them?
options: SafeWork NSW
Safe Work Australia
WorkSafe Queensland
Comcare
questions.explanation: Safe Work Australia is the national policy body that develops model WHS laws and codes of practice. Enforcement is the responsibility of each state/territory regulator (e.g. SafeWork NSW, WorkSafe Queensland).

questions.q: A labour hire worker is injured on a civil construction site. Who owes a primary WHS duty of care?
options: Only the labour hire company
Only the host employer (PCBU on site)
Both the labour hire company and the host employer PCBU — overlapping duties
Neither — labour hire workers are independent contractors
questions.explanation: Both the labour hire company and the host employer are PCBUs with overlapping duties. Each must ensure the worker's health and safety so far as reasonably practicable within their control and influence.

questions.q: An officer of a construction company is prosecuted for a WHS breach. The company is also being prosecuted for the same incident. Can both be held liable?
options: No — only one can be prosecuted for the same incident
Yes — officers have personal due diligence duties and can be prosecuted independently of any company prosecution
Only if the officer was physically on site at the time
Only if the company is first found guilty
questions.explanation: Officers have independent due diligence duties under s.27 of the WHS Act. They can be prosecuted personally and simultaneously with a PCBU prosecution — there is no requirement for the company to be convicted first.

questions.q: When assessing 'reasonably practicable' under the WHS Act, when should cost be considered?
options: First — it determines the budget for safety measures
Equally alongside all other factors
Last — only after considering likelihood, severity, knowledge, and control availability
Cost is not relevant to reasonably practicable
questions.explanation: Cost is the last factor in the reasonably practicable assessment — weighed only after considering likelihood, severity, knowledge, and availability of controls. A PCBU cannot justify inadequate safety controls simply because measures are expensive.

questions.q: Does the WHS Act apply to unpaid volunteers on a construction site?
options: Never — volunteers are excluded from the WHS Act
Yes — the definition of 'worker' can include volunteers in some circumstances
Only if the volunteer is registered with the PCBU
Only after 30 days of volunteering
questions.explanation: The WHS Act's definition of 'worker' can include volunteers in some circumstances — particularly those performing work that would otherwise be done by paid workers. PCBUs must ensure their health and safety while performing work.

lessons.title: Hierarchy of Controls, SWMS and JSA
lessons.content:
## Risk Management Under the WHS Act

Risk management is the operational process by which PCBUs fulfil their duty to ensure health and safety. The WHS Act requires a systematic approach: identify hazards, assess risks, implement controls, and review.

## Hazard Identification

Hazards must be identified before work begins — and continuously during work:
- **Design phase**: Eliminate hazards at design stage (buildings, processes, equipment)
- **Pre-start**: Daily site walk and equipment checks before work
- **SWMS preparation**: Systematic identification for high-risk construction work
- **Incident investigation**: Near misses reveal latent hazards
- **Worker consultation**: Workers often know hazards better than management

## Hierarchy of Controls (WHS Regulations)

The WHS Regulations require controls to be applied in priority order:

| Level | Control Type | Example — Civil Construction |
|-------|-------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Elimination** | Remove the hazardous task entirely |
| 2 | **Substitution** | Replace diesel-powered plant with electric |
| 3 | **Isolation** | Exclusion zone around moving plant |
| 4 | **Engineering** | Trench shoring, guarding, noise enclosures |
| 5 | **Administrative** | Safe work procedures, permits, training |
| 6 | **PPE** | Hard hat, hi-vis, steel caps — last resort |

PPE must be used WITH higher-level controls for high-risk activities — never as a standalone measure.

## Safe Work Method Statements (SWMS)

A **SWMS** is required for all **high-risk construction work (HRCW)** under the WHS Regulations.

**High-risk construction work includes:**
- Work involving a risk of a person falling more than 2 metres
- Demolition work
- Work involving, or likely to involve, disturbance of asbestos
- Excavation to a depth greater than 1.5 m
- Work in confined spaces
- Work on or near pressurised gas distribution mains or piping
- Work on or near energised electrical installations
- Work in, on, or adjacent to a road, railway, or other traffic
- Work on telecommunications towers

**SWMS must:**
- Identify the high-risk construction work
- State the hazards and risks associated with the work
- Describe the measures to control the risks
- Describe how those measures are implemented, monitored, and reviewed

**SWMS must be:**
- Prepared before high-risk construction work commences
- Readily accessible to workers performing the work
- Complied with by all workers
- Reviewed and revised if the work changes

## Job Safety Analysis (JSA)

A **JSA** (also called Job Hazard Analysis or JHA) is a task-based risk management tool:

**JSA Process:**
1. Select the task to be analysed
2. Break the task into sequential steps
3. Identify hazards at each step
4. Assess risk (likelihood × consequence)
5. Identify controls for each hazard (hierarchy of controls)
6. Assign responsibility and timeline
7. Review with workers who will perform the task
8. Workers sign off — acknowledging they understand the controls

JSAs are not legally required for all work (unlike SWMS for HRCW) but represent best practice for any non-routine or complex task.

## Consulting Workers — WHS Act Requirement

The WHS Act (s.47) requires PCBUs to consult workers on health and safety matters:
- Identifying and assessing hazards
- Decisions about controls
- Introducing new or changed systems of work
- Changing PPE requirements
- Resolving safety concerns

Worker consultation is not optional — it is a legal requirement. Workers performing the task often have the most accurate knowledge of the real hazards involved.

## Key Takeaways

- Hierarchy of controls: Elimination first — PPE last — never PPE alone for high risks
- SWMS required for all High-Risk Construction Work — must be prepared before work starts
- Excavation >1.5 m = HRCW in Australia — SWMS mandatory
- JSA: task-by-step hazard identification — best practice for all complex tasks
- Worker consultation is legally required under WHS Act s.47

questions.q: Excavation to a depth greater than 1.5 m is classified as what type of work under the WHS Regulations?
options: Notifiable work only
Standard construction work
High-Risk Construction Work (HRCW) requiring a SWMS
Confined space work only
questions.explanation: Excavation deeper than 1.5 m is classified as High-Risk Construction Work under the WHS Regulations. A Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS) must be prepared before this work commences.

questions.q: At what level of the hierarchy of controls does PPE sit?
options: Level 1 — most effective
Level 3 — after elimination and substitution
Level 6 — last resort
PPE is not part of the hierarchy
questions.explanation: PPE is Level 6 — the last and least effective control. It protects only the individual and provides no reduction of the hazard itself. For high-risk activities, PPE must be used WITH higher-level controls.

questions.q: A SWMS for high-risk construction work must be reviewed and revised when:
options: Annually regardless of changes
The work or site conditions change significantly
Only after an incident occurs
When the worker requests it
questions.explanation: A SWMS must be reviewed and revised if the work scope or site conditions change significantly. It must reflect the actual work being performed — an outdated SWMS provides no protection.

questions.q: Under WHS Act s.47, when must a PCBU consult with workers?
options: Only during the annual safety review
When identifying hazards, assessing risks, making decisions about controls, and changing work systems
Only if a union representative is present
Only after an incident
questions.explanation: WHS Act s.47 requires consultation when identifying hazards, assessing risks, making decisions about controls, changing work systems or PPE, and resolving safety concerns. Consultation is a legal requirement — not optional.

questions.q: What is the primary difference between a SWMS and a JSA?
options: A JSA is legally required for HRCW; a SWMS is voluntary
A SWMS is legally required for all HRCW; a JSA is best practice for complex tasks but not always legally mandated
They are identical documents with different names
A SWMS is only used for confined space work
questions.explanation: A SWMS is legally required under the WHS Regulations for all High-Risk Construction Work. A JSA is a best-practice task-based risk management tool — highly recommended for complex tasks but not always a specific legal requirement.

lessons.title: High-Risk Work Licences, White Card and Site Induction
lessons.content:
## Site Induction Requirements in Australia

Before any worker begins work on an Australian construction site, they must complete a site induction. Additionally, working in construction requires a General Construction Induction (White Card) and, for specific activities, a High-Risk Work (HRW) Licence.

## White Card — General Construction Induction Training

The **White Card** (formally: General Construction Induction Training certificate) is mandatory for ALL workers entering a construction site in Australia:
- Covers fundamental WHS knowledge for construction
- Completed with a Registered Training Organisation (RTO)
- Nationally recognised — valid in all Australian states/territories
- No expiry — but refresher training recommended after long absence

**White Card content includes:**
- WHS legal framework and duty holders
- Hazard identification and risk management
- Common construction hazards and controls
- Emergency procedures
- Worker rights and WHS consultation

**Verification:** Site managers must verify each worker's White Card before they start work. Workers without a valid White Card must NOT be permitted on site.

## High-Risk Work Licences

Certain high-risk activities require a specific **High-Risk Work (HRW) Licence** from the state/territory regulator:

| HRW Licence Class | Activity |
|-------------------|---------|
| **CN, C2, C6** | Cranes (tower, mobile, etc.) |
| **DG** | Dogging — attaching loads to cranes |
| **RB** | Rigging — basic |
| **RI** | Rigging — intermediate |
| **RA** | Rigging — advanced |
| **EWP** | Elevating work platform > 11 m boom height |
| **FL** | Forklift operation |
| **BF** | Boom-type elevating work platform |

**How to obtain an HRW Licence:**
1. Complete training with a licensed trainer/assessor
2. Pass practical and knowledge assessments
3. Apply to the state/territory regulator (e.g. SafeWork NSW) with evidence
4. Licence issued — valid for 5 years, must be renewed

**Civil construction implications:** Any crane lifts, EWP operations, or dogging activities on a civil site require workers with appropriate HRW licences. The PCBU must verify licence classes before assigning work.

## Site-Specific Induction

In addition to the White Card, every worker must receive a **site-specific induction** before starting work:

**Site induction must cover:**
- Site layout: entry/exit points, amenities, assembly areas
- Site hazards and specific controls
- Emergency procedures: evacuation routes, muster points, first aid locations
- Site rules: PPE requirements, speed limits, no-go zones
- Reporting structure: supervisor contacts, incident reporting process
- Environmental controls: dust, water, waste management

**Documentation:** Site induction must be documented — signed attendance record. Workers must not enter the active site before induction is complete.

## Principal Contractor Obligations

On construction projects with a value above the threshold, a **Principal Contractor (PC)** must:
- Prepare a Construction Work Health and Safety Management Plan (WHS Management Plan)
- Ensure all contractors comply with WHS requirements
- Coordinate WHS across all workers on the project
- Display the WHS Management Plan on site

## Key Takeaways

- White Card: mandatory for ALL workers on Australian construction sites — nationally recognised, no expiry
- HRW Licence required for cranes, rigging, dogging, EWPs, forklifts — 5-year validity
- Site induction: must occur before any worker enters the active site — document with signed record
- Principal Contractor: manages WHS across the project — WHS Management Plan required above threshold value

questions.q: What is the White Card and who is required to have one on Australian construction sites?
options: A site access card issued by the principal contractor — required for supervisors only
General Construction Induction Training certificate — required for ALL workers entering any construction site
A qualification card for plant operators — renewed every 5 years
An identity document issued by the state regulator — required for new workers only
questions.explanation: The White Card is the General Construction Induction Training certificate — mandatory for every worker entering any Australian construction site. It is nationally recognised and has no expiry (though refreshers are recommended after long absence).

questions.q: What HRW Licence class is required to operate an elevating work platform with a boom height exceeding 11 metres?
options: FL (forklift)
DG (dogging)
EWP or BF (elevating work platform)
RB (rigging basic)
questions.explanation: An EWP or BF (boom-type EWP) High-Risk Work Licence is required to operate an elevating work platform with a boom height exceeding 11 metres. Operating without the correct HRW licence is a WHS breach.

questions.q: When must a site-specific induction be completed?
options: Within the first week of starting on site
Before the worker enters the active construction site
At any point during the first shift
Only for workers who have never worked in construction before
questions.explanation: Site-specific induction must be completed BEFORE the worker enters the active construction site. Workers must not access the site prior to induction — the site manager must verify this for every new worker.

questions.q: How long is a High-Risk Work Licence valid in Australia?
options: 1 year
3 years
5 years
Indefinitely
questions.explanation: High-Risk Work Licences are valid for 5 years and must be renewed before expiry. The PCBU should track licence expiry dates to ensure all licensed workers remain current.

lessons.title: Pre-Starts, Maintenance, Plant Registration and Operator Competency
lessons.content:
## Plant and Equipment Safety in Australian Civil Construction

Mobile plant — excavators, graders, rollers, compactors, cranes — creates some of the highest fatality risks in civil construction. The WHS Regulations impose specific requirements for the management of plant to protect operators and ground workers.

## Plant Registration

Certain 'items of plant' must be **registered** with the state/territory WHS regulator before use:

**Registerable plant examples:**
- Cranes and hoists with a safe working load > 10 kN
- Pressure vessels (steam, compressed air)
- Boilers
- Lifts (passenger and goods)
- Mobile cranes > 10 t
- Tower cranes
- Amusement devices

**Note:** Standard excavators, rollers, and compactors typically do NOT require plant registration — but must still meet maintenance, inspection, and operator competency requirements.

## Designs of Plant

Plant designs must also be registered for certain plant items:
- New design of registerable plant must be registered with a regulator
- Provides assurance the design has been assessed for safety
- Design registration number must be recorded in the plant item registration

## Pre-Start Inspections

Daily pre-start inspections are mandatory for all mobile plant on Australian civil sites:

**Pre-start inspection checklist (excavator example):**
- Engine oil, coolant, hydraulic fluid, fuel levels
- Hydraulic hoses — leaks, chafing, damage
- Undercarriage — track tension, rollers, idlers, sprockets
- Attachments — secure pins, bucket/teeth condition
- Cab — seat belt, ROPS, controls function, mirrors
- Warning devices — reverse alarm, lights, horn
- Surroundings — clear working area before start-up

**Documentation:** Pre-start records must be kept — typically 1–3 month retention minimum. Defects must be reported to the supervisor and plant taken out of service until repaired.

## Maintenance Regime

PCBUs must implement a plant maintenance program:
- Manufacturer's recommended service intervals (e.g. 250 h, 500 h, 1000 h services)
- Maintenance records kept for each item of plant
- Major repairs authorised by a competent person
- Critical safety components (brakes, ROPS, lifting attachments) must not be bypassed or disabled

## Operator Competency

The WHS Regulations require that plant is only operated by **competent persons**:

| Plant Type | Competency Evidence |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Excavator | RII training package units (e.g. RIIMPO320F) |
| Crane | HRW Licence (CN, C2, C6 etc.) |
| EWP >11m | HRW Licence (EWP/BF) |
| Dogging | HRW Licence (DG) |
| Forklift | HRW Licence (FL) |
| Roller/compactor | RII training package units |

**Competency verification process:**
1. Request evidence of training/licence from worker
2. Verify currency (HRW licences expire every 5 years)
3. Conduct site-specific plant induction
4. Formally authorise the worker for specific plant on site
5. Record authorisation

## Safe Design of Work Areas for Plant

- Designate pedestrian exclusion zones around all operating plant
- Separate pedestrian and plant traffic routes where possible
- All reversing movements of mobile plant require either a spotter or a functioning reversing camera
- Audit compliance — randomly check that exclusion zones are being observed

## Key Takeaways

- Cranes and pressure vessels require plant registration — excavators typically do not but still have strict requirements
- Pre-start inspections: daily, documented, defects reported and plant taken out of service
- Competent persons: RII training units for civil plant, HRW licences for cranes/EWP/dogging
- Maintenance program: follow manufacturer intervals — keep records
- Exclusion zones and spotters: mandatory for reversing plant and operating near workers

questions.q: Which of the following items of plant typically requires registration with the WHS regulator?
options: A 20-tonne excavator
A vibratory roller
A tower crane
A plate compactor
questions.explanation: Tower cranes are registerable plant under the WHS Regulations — they must be registered with the state/territory regulator before use. Standard excavators, rollers, and compactors typically do not require registration but must meet other safety requirements.

questions.q: What training package provides competency units for excavator operators in Australia?
options: CPP (Construction, Plumbing and Services)
RII (Resources and Infrastructure Industry)
CPC (Construction, Plumbing and Services)
TLI (Transport and Logistics)
questions.explanation: The RII (Resources and Infrastructure Industry) training package provides competency units for civil plant operation, including RIIMPO320F for excavators. These are nationally recognised units within the Australian Qualifications Framework.

questions.q: Pre-start inspection records for mobile plant should be:
options: Kept only when a defect is found
Completed and documented before each shift, with defects reported and plant taken out of service
Completed weekly by the site supervisor
Only required for registered plant
questions.explanation: Pre-start inspections must be completed before every shift, documented, and defects reported. Plant with defects must be taken out of service until repaired. Records are retained as evidence of due diligence.

questions.q: A reversing excavator has a malfunctioning reverse alarm. What is the minimum acceptable control?
options: Continue using the machine — the operator has good mirrors
Use a dedicated spotter with clear communication with the operator during all reversing movements
Place a sign at the back of the machine warning workers
Restrict reversing speed to walking pace only
questions.explanation: When a reverse alarm is malfunctioning, a dedicated spotter must be used for all reversing movements. The spotter provides equivalent warning to workers. The defect should also be reported and the alarm repaired as soon as possible.

lessons.title: Serious Injuries, SafeWork Notifications and Return to Work
lessons.content:
## Incident Reporting and Management in Australia

Effective incident reporting is both a legal obligation and a critical safety improvement mechanism. Under the WHS Act and Regulations, certain incidents must be reported to the regulator immediately.

## Notifiable Incidents

PCBUs must notify the WHS regulator immediately of **notifiable incidents**:

| Incident Type | Definition |
|--------------|-----------|
| **Notifiable death** | Death of a worker or other person from work-related incident |
| **Notifiable serious injury or illness** | Requires immediate treatment as an in-patient in hospital; amputation; serious head, eye or ear injury; serious burns; serious spinal injury; loss of a bodily function; serious lacerations |
| **Dangerous incident** | Near miss with potential for serious injury — collapse of excavation, uncontrolled spill of hazardous substance, explosion, fire |

**Notification requirements:**
- Notify the regulator by the **fastest means possible** (phone call)
- Written notification within 48 hours of dangerous incident; immediately for deaths/serious injuries
- Preserve the incident site — do NOT disturb until regulator authorises

**Regulator notification contacts:**
- SafeWork NSW: 13 10 50
- WorkSafe Queensland: 1300 362 128
- SafeWork SA: 1300 365 255
- WorkSafe WA: 1300 307 877

## Scene Preservation

After a notifiable incident, the scene must be preserved:
- Do not move, interfere with, or disturb the site
- Exception: to assist an injured person, make the site safe, or comply with regulator direction
- Document the scene with photographs and measurements before preservation ends
- Regulator inspector will attend and conduct investigation

## Incident Investigation

All incidents and near misses should be investigated — not just notifiable events:

**Investigation process:**
1. **Immediate response**: Secure scene, treat injured, notify emergency services if needed
2. **Regulator notification** (if notifiable event)
3. **Gather evidence**: Photos, measurements, witness statements, CCTV, plant records
4. **Root cause analysis**: Identify immediate cause, contributing factors, and root causes
5. **Corrective actions**: Identify controls to prevent recurrence
6. **Communication**: Share learnings with the site and wider organisation
7. **Review**: Monitor effectiveness of corrective actions

**Root cause analysis tools:**
- 5 Whys: Ask 'why' five times to reach root cause
- Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram: Categorise causes (People, Plant, Process, Environment)
- Bow-tie analysis: Map hazard → barriers → consequences

## Return to Work (RTW)

Workers injured at work are entitled to return to work support under workers' compensation schemes:
- **Workers' compensation**: State-based — WorkCover NSW, WorkCover QLD, WorkCover SA etc.
- **RTW plan**: Developed with the injured worker, treating doctor, and employer
- **Suitable duties**: Temporary modified work within medical capacity
- **Early intervention**: Contact injured worker early — return to work sooner improves outcomes

PCBUs have an obligation to offer suitable work to injured workers — failure can result in workers' compensation premium increases and potential claims.

## Key Takeaways

- Notifiable incidents: death, serious injury, dangerous incident — notify regulator by fastest means
- Preserve the scene after a notifiable event — do not disturb without regulator authorisation
- Investigate ALL incidents and near misses — near misses are leading indicators
- Root cause analysis: 5 Whys, fishbone — find systemic causes, not just immediate causes
- Return to work: legal obligation — early intervention with suitable duties improves outcomes

questions.q: An excavator bucket strikes and severs a water main, causing the trench to flood. Workers escape uninjured. Is this a notifiable incident?
options: No — no workers were injured
Yes — an uncontrolled release creating a dangerous situation is a notifiable dangerous incident even with no injuries
Only if the flood is above 500 mm deep
Only if the water main was pressurised above 150 kPa
questions.explanation: A dangerous incident is notifiable even with no injuries — it includes uncontrolled releases and collapses with potential for serious injury. The PCBU must notify the regulator by fastest means and preserve the scene.

questions.q: After a notifiable incident, when can the site be disturbed or cleaned up?
options: Immediately after the injured person is treated
After 24 hours
Only when authorised by the WHS regulator inspector
When the site supervisor deems it safe to continue
questions.explanation: The incident site must be preserved and must not be disturbed until the WHS regulator authorises disturbance. The only exceptions are assisting an injured person, making the site safe from immediate danger, or complying with a specific regulator direction.

questions.q: What does the '5 Whys' technique achieve in incident investigation?
options: Allocates blame to 5 specific workers
Identifies 5 corrective actions for every incident
Progressively uncovers root causes by asking 'why' at each causal level
Categorises causes across 5 standard categories
questions.explanation: The 5 Whys technique asks 'why did this happen?' repeatedly (typically 5 times) to move beyond the immediate/surface cause to the underlying systemic root cause. Addressing root causes prevents recurrence.

questions.q: What is the purpose of a Return to Work (RTW) plan?
options: To return the worker to their pre-injury role as quickly as possible regardless of medical status
To provide a documented plan for the injured worker's gradual return to suitable work within their medical capacity
To document the incident for insurance purposes
To identify whether the worker is fit to continue employment
questions.explanation: A RTW plan provides a structured, documented approach for returning the injured worker to suitable work progressively within their medical capacity. Early return to suitable duties improves recovery outcomes and is a legal obligation for the PCBU.

questions.q: How quickly must a PCBU notify the WHS regulator after a worker sustains a serious injury requiring immediate in-patient hospital treatment?
options: Within 48 hours in writing
Within 7 days via online form
Immediately by the fastest means practicable (e.g. phone)
Within 24 hours
questions.explanation: Serious injuries and deaths must be notified IMMEDIATELY by the fastest means practicable — typically by phone to the state regulator. Written notification follows but the immediate phone notification is the critical legal requirement.
